original_hypothesis.title: Cumulative Permission Rule Usage Never Persisted to Storage
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In PermissionValidator.validatePermission, when cumulative rules are processed, the computed cumulative usage amount is assigned to a local memory variable (`usageLimit.usageAmount = value256`) but this update never propagates back to the `newUsageLimits` array. Solidity memory struct assignment creates copies, so after `newUsageLimits[j] = usageLimit` the array entry and local variable are independent. The returned array always contains `usageAmount = 0` for cumulative entries. This flows to `_validateLimitUsageIncrement` which constructs the expected `incrementUsageLimit` call with `usageAmount = 0`. Consequently, `incrementUsageLimit` writes 0 to storage, meaning cumulative limits are never actually recorded. A session key with a cumulative spending limit of X can spend up to X per transaction, indefinitely across unlimited transactions.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a code-level memory semantics bug that completely defeats cumulative permission enforcement. Session keys designed to have lifetime spending caps become uncapped. The bug is deterministic and requires no special conditions beyond a session key with cumulative rules.

proof_of_signal.pattern: usageLimit = UsageLimit({...}); newUsageLimits[j] = usageLimit; ... usageLimit.usageAmount = value256; // local copy only, array not updated

preconditions: Wallet has session key configured with cumulative permission rules
Session key holder wants to exceed cumulative limit
At least one cumulative ParameterRule exists in the Permission

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: newUsageLimits[j] = usageLimit (copy); later usageLimit.usageAmount = value256 (updates local, not array)
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Each transaction independently reads storage (always 0), validates against rule.value, and writes 0 back to storage via incrementUsageLimit
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Session key with cumulative limit of 1 ETH can transfer up to 1 ETH per transaction, unlimited total. With 100 txs = 100 ETH stolen

economic_questions: What is the typical cumulative limit configured for session keys in production?
How quickly can a session key submit multiple transactions before detection?
Are there other rate-limiting mechanisms outside the smart contract?
validation_requirements: Construct a test showing storage remains 0 after incrementUsageLimit with cumulative rules
Execute two sequential transactions both using the full cumulative limit
Verify the usageAmount in returned newUsageLimits array is always 0

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: 0 (session key holder uses their existing session permissions)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas costs per transaction (~0.001 ETH per tx on L2)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to entire wallet balance, limited only by per-call rule.value
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Multiple transactions, each within a few seconds

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time while session key is valid

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Wallet owners who grant session keys with cumulative limits
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Full value of assets accessible within per-call limits
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All assets held by wallets using cumulative session permissions

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Verify Solidity memory copy semantics for struct assignment in validatePermission
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace usageAmount value from validatePermission return through _validateLimitUsageIncrement to incrementUsageLimit

validation_result.impact_summary: No defect exists. Solidity memory-to-memory struct assignments create references, not copies, so the usageLimit update propagates correctly to the newUsageLimits array.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis claims that `usageLimit.usageAmount = value256` only updates a local copy and never propagates back to `newUsageLimits[j]`. This is based on the incorrect premise that 'Solidity memory struct assignment creates copies.' In reality, Solidity documentation explicitly states: 'Assignments from memory to memory only create references. This means that changes to one memory variable are also visible in all other memory variables that refer to the same data.' Since UsageLimit is a struct (a reference type), both `newUsageLimits[j] = usageLimit` and `usageLimit = newUsageLimits[j]` create aliases, not copies. After either assignment, both variables point to the same memory location, so `usageLimit.usageAmount = value256` is immediately visible through `newUsageLimits[j].usageAmount`.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal at PermissionValidator.sol:75-110 correctly identifies the code pattern but incorrectly characterizes the semantics. Lines 87-92 show `usageLimit = UsageLimit({...}); newUsageLimits[j] = usageLimit;` which makes both point to the same memory struct. Line 95 shows `usageLimit = newUsageLimits[j];` in the existing-entry case, which also creates a reference. Line 103 `usageLimit.usageAmount = value256;` modifies the shared memory location in both cases, correctly updating the array.

calling_sequence: 2. For cumulative rules, enters the cumulative block (line 78)
3. NEW ENTRY PATH: usageLimit = UsageLimit({...}) allocates new struct at memory pointer P (line 87-88)
4. newUsageLimits[j] = usageLimit copies pointer P into array slot j (line 89) - REFERENCE, not copy
5. OR EXISTING PATH: usageLimit = newUsageLimits[j] copies pointer from array slot into usageLimit (line 95) - REFERENCE
6. usageLimit.usageAmount = value256 (line 103) writes to memory at pointer P, visible through both variables
7. newUsageLimits returned correctly contains the updated usageAmount
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: No vulnerable path exists. The code correctly relies on Solidity's memory reference semantics for struct types. In both the new-entry and existing-entry cases, usageLimit and newUsageLimits[j] point to the same memory struct. The assignment at line 103 is visible through the array.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment configuration relevant to this hypothesis.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is invalid because it relies on a misunderstanding of Solidity memory semantics. Memory-to-memory assignments of reference types (structs, arrays) create references/aliases, not independent copies. The `usageLimit.usageAmount = value256` update at PermissionValidator.sol:103 IS visible through `newUsageLimits[j]` because both variables point to the same memory location. The cumulative usage tracking works as intended: the updated usage amounts flow through _validateLimitUsageIncrement to incrementUsageLimit, which persists them to storage.
validation_result.impact_assessment: No impact. The code is correct. Cumulative permission limits are properly tracked and persisted. A session key configured with a cumulative spending limit of X can only spend X total across all transactions, as designed.
validation_result.replication_plan: Write a Foundry test that: (1) configures a session key with a cumulative rule limiting total transfers to 1 ETH, (2) executes a first transaction transferring 0.5 ETH, (3) verifies storage shows 0.5 ETH used via getLimitUsage, (4) executes a second transaction transferring 0.5 ETH, (5) verifies storage shows 1.0 ETH used, (6) attempts a third transaction transferring any amount and confirms it reverts due to exceeding the cumulative limit.
uncertainties: The analysis is based on well-documented Solidity memory semantics. The only uncertainty would be if the Solidity compiler had a bug contradicting its documented behavior, which is extremely unlikely for such a fundamental feature.
additional_testing_needed: A concrete test with cumulative rules to empirically confirm the storage tracking across multiple transactions would provide definitive proof, though the Solidity language specification is clear on this point.
